Edition 03 · 2026
02 · Design

Logo Brief

How to read this document

This is the deep creative brief for the Maxxim logomark, produced in the Design phase against Master Briefing v2.1, the Gate 1 decisions in the Discovery Addendum, and the 1 July refresh that folded in the 29 June product mechanics and the live Jewell partner site. It takes the strategic Visual Identity Direction from brand-strategy.html and turns it into something a designer, human or the Design Studio, can generate against without guessing. It does not re-open the visual identity. The identity is locked, and this brief works inside it.

What is locked, and not up for discussion in this brief: Signal Blue #2D5BFF as the brand colour, Poppins as the typeface, design Edition 03 as the current system edition, and the removal of the Blue Frog motif. The brief expresses the brand inside those constraints. Where a choice is still Clent's, it is offered as an option and flagged, not decided.

What is new in this version. A reversed wordmark now exists as the brand's inverse variant: the white Maxxim logotype on transparent, for dark grounds and Signal Blue fields. It is a variant added inside the locked identity, not a re-opening of it. This brief now specifies its purpose, clear space, minimum size, contrast and accessibility notes, and where it is used against the primary mark. The reversed variant lives at https://assets.maxxim.ai/maxxim/inverse/78512f900b9d.svg and is referenced in section 7.

1. The mark in one line

A clean, typographic mark that reads as a platform and a standard, the proven method run by a human at AI speed, not a mascot and not a black box.

2. Brand context

Maxxim is the commercialisation engine that lets trusted operators run Jewell's proven, gated 3D Process at AI speed, with an accountable human at every gate. Its primary customer is the Capacity-Capped Operator, the Client Partner who can win the work but cannot deliver agency-grade growth repeatably. The personality is a Sage, weighted toward authority, proof, and judgment, with a Creator support note as the builder of an engine, and a warmth reserved for how the partner community is spoken about. The brand idea the mark must serve is "the proven method, run by a human, at AI speed", held together by a community rather than a lone operator. Voice is direct, grounded, credible, and calm. The brand sells finished, owned outcomes, not spectacle.

Two facts about how the brand now shows up in market sharpen the mark's job. First, the engine is legible through three named specialists: Max does the words, Sal does the pixels in the Design Studio, and Pip runs the Social Studio. The logo is the platform they sit under, so it must read as the parent standard those specialists work to, never as one of them. Second, the seam the brand runs on, the human 20 percent and the machine 80 percent, is now proven by a live partner. Jewell is in market on Maxxim as the engine behind it, the name on the door with Maxxim the engine behind it. The mark's job is to be that engine mark: quiet enough to sit behind a partner's own brand, strong enough to stand as a standard the partner trusts. It is the platform mark, not the partner's mark.

3. What the logo must do

Communication goals first, then the functional requirements that become the variant set.

Communication goals. Signal trust and competence at a glance, which is the ground the trust gap is fought on. Read as a platform and a standard, calm and precise, the Sage restraint made visible. Carry a quiet sense of speed and modernity through Signal Blue and clean geometry, without tipping into a generic tech or AI look. Leave room to imply the gated 3D method and the human moat without literal illustration. Sit comfortably as the engine behind a partner's own brand, since the mark often appears alongside or beneath a partner rather than as the headline.

Functional requirements. The brand is digital-first, so the mark has to survive small and single-colour.

  • Must read at favicon size, down to 16 by 16 pixels in a browser tab.
  • Must work in a single colour, Signal Blue on white and white on Signal Blue, with no loss of legibility.
  • Must hold up reversed on a dark background, on a Signal Blue field, and on a busy end-client context behind it. This is the job the inverse variant now exists to do.
  • Must work as an app icon, a partner-hub avatar, and a social avatar inside a circle and a rounded square crop.
  • Must appear in print as well as screen, so it has to reproduce in flat single-colour with no gradient, glow, or photographic effect.

These contexts define the variant set later: primary lockup, single-colour, inverse (the reversed wordmark), brandmark, and icon.

4. Form and concept direction

Type of mark. A combination mark built from a Poppins-based wordmark plus a compact, geometric brandmark that can stand alone as the icon. With the Blue Frog gone, the system leans typographic, so the wordmark carries most of the identity and the brandmark is restrained and abstract, never a character.

Form language. Geometric, even-weighted, and confident. Straight lines and clean curves consistent with Poppins, generous internal spacing, strong negative space, and a single idea per mark. No ornament. Emphasis comes from weight and structure, not decoration, which mirrors the voice that strips out exclamation marks and leans on the strength of the claim. High contrast and legibility over cleverness.

Device to carry forward, and one to retire. Carry forward Signal Blue and the typographic, standard-like restraint of Edition 03. Deliberately retire the Blue Frog motif. Do not reintroduce it in any form, literal or stylised, and do not replace it with a different animal, creature, or mascot. The mark is a platform, not a character.

Two or three concept territories to explore.

  • Territory A, the gated M. A monogram built on the M, where the strokes read as steps or gates, the three Ds of the method (Discover, Design, Deploy) implied by an even rhythm of verticals. This carries the gated method without spelling it out. Most ownable, most aligned to the proof story.
  • Territory B, the signal. An abstract mark expressing "Signal", clarity rather than noise, a clean upward or forward motion that reads as speed and direction. This leans on the colour name and the trust-plus-speed promise. Risk to manage, keep it away from generic arrow or chevron clichés.
  • Territory C, the wordmark-led lettermark. A pure typographic treatment of Maxxim in Poppins, with a single refined detail in one letterform, for example the crossing of the double x, doing the brandmark work. Cleanest, most Sage, most defensible at small sizes. The safe, strong baseline if A and B over-reach.

The double x in Maxxim is a distinctive asset worth exploring across all three territories, a natural place for a precise, structural detail that does not become a gimmick. Whichever territory is chosen must survive the reversal test cleanly, because the reversed wordmark is now a first-class variant, not an afterthought. A detail that reads on white but disappears or muddies when knocked out to white on Signal Blue is a detail to rework, not ship.

5. Colour direction

Colour is locked, not provisional. The mark is built in Signal Blue, full stop. No secondary brand colour is introduced here, and none is invented.

RoleValueStatusUse
Brand primary, Signal Blue#2D5BFFLockedThe mark on white, and the field colour for reversed lockups.
Reverse, white#FFFFFFLockedThe reversed wordmark, knocked out to white on Signal Blue or on a dark field. This is the inverse variant.
Single-colour dark, near-blackProvisional, confirmed at Brand IdentityProvisionalFor mono print and single-colour contexts where blue is unavailable. A near-black, not pure black, to stay calm rather than harsh.

No gradients, no glows, no two-tone treatment of the mark. The palette stays disciplined and high-contrast, in keeping with a Sage brand that values legibility over decoration. The colour psychology the strategy already settled holds: blue for trust, reliability, and competence, the saturated electric quality for speed and modernity, and the Signal name for clarity over noise. The reversed variant is a colour treatment of the locked identity, white out of Signal Blue or a dark ground, not a new colour and not a new mark.

6. Typography direction, wordmark and lockup

The wordmark is set in Poppins, locked. Use a weight with presence and evenness, a medium to semibold range, refined for optical balance at logo scale rather than dropped in raw. Letter-spacing kept tight and deliberate, generous clear space around it. The word is Maxxim, never Joule and never any corruption of the name. Where the method owner is referenced in a lockup, it is Jewell.

Lockups required. The Studio and the refining designer must produce all three, not just the mark, and each must exist positive, reversed, and mono.

  • Horizontal lockup, brandmark left, Maxxim wordmark right. The primary, default lockup for the website header and the partner hub.
  • Stacked lockup, brandmark above, wordmark below, centred. For square and vertical contexts and for the showcase.
  • Icon only, the brandmark alone, for favicon, app icon, and social avatar.

7. The reversed variant, the inverse mark

A reversed wordmark now exists as the brand's inverse variant: the white Maxxim logotype on transparent. It carries the same locked identity, Signal Blue, Poppins, Edition 03, expressed for dark grounds. It is a variant, not a new mark, and not a re-opening of the identity.

Where it lives: https://assets.maxxim.ai/maxxim/inverse/78512f900b9d.svg. It is an SVG, so it scales cleanly, and it is transparent, so it reverses out of whatever field it is placed on rather than carrying its own background.

Purpose, what it is for. The reversed variant exists so the mark holds contrast where the primary Signal-Blue-on-white mark would fail or disappear. Use it, and only it, in these cases.

  • On the brand's own Signal Blue field (#2D5BFF), where the mark is knocked out to white. This is the signature reversed lockup, the brand's colour carrying the white wordmark.
  • On dark grounds, near-black sections, dark hero panels, dark partner-hub surfaces, and dark app or presentation themes.
  • Over dark imagery or a busy dark end-client context, where a solid legible white wordmark reads and a coloured mark would compete with the photo.

Where it is not used. Do not place the reversed variant on white, on light grounds, on light or mid-tone imagery, or on a Signal Blue tint too pale to hold white. On those, use the primary mark. The reversed variant is not a general-purpose swap; it is the dark-ground answer only.

Clear space and minimum size. The reversed variant follows the same protection rules as the primary mark. Clear space is x on every side, where x is the cap-height of the wordmark, and nothing may enter that border. Minimum sizes match the primary set: the horizontal lockup at 120 pixels wide on screen, or 24 millimetres in print, and the stacked lockup at 80 pixels or 18 millimetres. Below the lockup floor, drop to the reversed icon rather than letting the white wordmark go illegible. Because white detail thins out visually faster than dark detail, test the reversed variant at the floor sizes specifically and thicken any fine strokes that close up or vanish against the field.

Contrast and accessibility. The reversed variant carries the contrast rule for every dark surface the brand uses.

  • On Signal Blue #2D5BFF, white #FFFFFF gives a contrast ratio of roughly 4.7 to 1, which clears the WCAG 2.1 AA threshold of 3 to 1 for graphical objects and large text, and is the intended pairing.
  • On any darker ground than Signal Blue, near-black or dark grey, contrast only increases, so those are safe.
  • Do not place the reversed variant on a Signal Blue that has been tinted lighter, or on a light or mid photo, because contrast falls below the threshold and legibility and accessibility both suffer. Where a ground is uncertain, add a dark scrim behind the mark or switch to the primary mark on a light panel.
  • The alt text for the embedded reversed mark is "Maxxim reversed logo", so assistive technology names it correctly wherever it is placed in HTML.

The reversed variant is one of the required colour treatments of each lockup, not a separate logo family. Every lockup, horizontal, stacked, and icon, exists in positive (Signal Blue on white), reverse (white on Signal Blue or dark, the inverse variant), and single-colour mono.

8. Lockup, clear space, and minimum size

The mark needs protected clear space and a tested floor so it stays legible everywhere the brand is digital-first. The sketch below is direction for the refining designer, not final geometry, and it holds for both the positive and the reversed variants.

9. Usage map, primary, reversed, and mark-only

Which form and which colour treatment goes where. This is the map the refining designer builds the variant set against, and it now names the reversed variant explicitly against the primary mark.
